name: 1. Java Basics & Fundamentals

subTopics.name: 1.1 Data Types & Variables
subTopics.description:
# Variables and Data Types
Java is statically-typed, meaning all variables must first be declared before they can be used.

### Primitive Data Types
| Type | Size | Range/Values |
|---|---|---|
| `byte` | 1 byte | -128 to 127 |
| `short` | 2 bytes | -32,768 to 32,767 |
| `int` | 4 bytes | -2^31 to 2^31-1 |
| `long` | 8 bytes | -2^63 to 2^63-1 |
| `float` | 4 bytes | Single-precision 32-bit IEEE 754 |
| `double` | 8 bytes | Double-precision 64-bit IEEE 754 |
| `char` | 2 bytes | 0 to 65,535 (Unicode) |
| `boolean`| 1 bit | `true` or `false` |

### Code Example
```java
int age = 25;
double price = 19.99;
char grade = 'A';
boolean isValid = true;
```

subTopics.name: 1.2 Operators
subTopics.description:
# Operators in Java
Java provides a rich set of operators to manipulate variables.

### 1. Arithmetic Operators
`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`

### 2. Relational Operators
`==`, `!=`, `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`

### 3. Logical Operators
`&&` (Logical AND), `||` (Logical OR), `!` (Logical NOT)

### 4. Bitwise Operators
`&`, `|`, `^`, `~`, `<<`, `>>`, `>>>` (Unsigned right shift)

### 5. Ternary Operator
```java
int max = (a > b) ? a : b;
```

name: 3. Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)
description:
# Object-Oriented Programming
Java's core paradigm is OOP. It revolves around the concept of "objects", which contain data (attributes) and code (methods).

### The 4 Pillars of OOP
1. **Encapsulation**: Hiding internal state and requiring all interaction to be performed through an object's methods.
2. **Inheritance**: A mechanism where a new class inherits properties and behaviors from an existing class.
3. **Polymorphism**: The ability of an object to take on many forms (Overloading and Overriding).
4. **Abstraction**: Hiding complex implementation details and showing only the essential features.

subTopics.name: 3.1 Classes, Objects & Constructors
3.2 Inheritance & Polymorphism
3.3 Interfaces & Abstract Classes
3.4 Keywords: static, final, this, super
subTopics.description:
# Important Keywords

### `static`
Belongs to the class, not instances. Shared among all objects.
```java
class Counter {
    static int count = 0; // Shared across all instances
}
```

### `final`
- **Variable**: Cannot be reassigned (acts as a constant).
- **Method**: Cannot be overridden by subclasses.
- **Class**: Cannot be inherited.

### `this`
Refers to the current object instance.
```java
public void setName(String name) {
    this.name = name; // Resolves shadowing
}
```

### `super`
Refers to the parent class instance (used to call parent constructor or overridden methods).
```java
super(); // Calls parent constructor
super.methodName();
```

name: 5. Collections Framework
description:
# The Collections Framework
A unified architecture for representing and manipulating collections (groups of objects).

subTopics.name: 5.1 Lists
5.2 Sets
subTopics.description:
# Sets
Collections that do not allow duplicate elements.

### HashSet
Backed by a HashMap. No guaranteed order. `O(1)` operations.
```java
Set<Integer> set = new HashSet<>();
set.add(1);
set.add(1); // Ignored
```

### TreeSet
Backed by a TreeMap (Red-Black Tree). Elements are sorted in natural order. `O(log n)` operations.
```java
Set<Integer> set = new TreeSet<>();
set.add(5);
set.add(2); // Iterator will return 2 then 5
```

name: 8. Java 8+ Features

# Modern Java
Java 8 introduced functional programming concepts, significantly changing how Java code is written.

subTopics.name: 8.1 Lambda Expressions
8.2 Streams API
subTopics.description:
# Streams API
Used to process collections of objects in a declarative way.
- **Intermediate Operations**: return a new stream (`filter`, `map`, `sorted`). Lazy execution.
- **Terminal Operations**: return a non-stream result (`collect`, `forEach`, `reduce`, `count`). Triggers execution.

### Example
```java
List<Integer> numbers = Arrays.asList(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6);
int sumOfSquaresOfEvens = numbers.stream()
    .filter(n -> n % 2 == 0)   // Keep evens: 2, 4, 6
    .map(n -> n * n)           // Square them: 4, 16, 36
    .reduce(0, Integer::sum);  // Sum them up: 56
```

subTopics.name: 8.3 Optional
